zones.label: WiFi 802.11 b/g/n
zones.summary: High-speed internet link via an access point
facts: Frequency: 2.4 GHz (all three standards); also 5 GHz (802.11n, not on Pico W)
Typical range: 30–50 m indoors; up to 100 m outdoors (open area)
Data speed: up to 150 Mbps (802.11n); real throughput 5–20 Mbps typical
Power use: medium–high — ~250 mA peak during transmit
Topology: star — all devices connect to a WiFi access point (router)
Protocol stack: TCP/IP; HTTP, HTTPS, MQTT, WebSocket
MicroPython boards: Raspberry Pi Pico W (CYW43439); ESP32 (built-in)
Best for: web servers, REST APIs, streaming data, cloud IoT with MQTT

zones.label: Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)
facts: Frequency: 2.4 GHz (40 channels, 2 MHz spacing)
Typical range: up to 40 m (BLE 5.0); up to 400 m (BLE 5 long-range mode)
Data speed: 2 Mbps (BLE 5.0); 1 Mbps (BLE 4.2)
Power use: very low — ~15–20 mA TX; ~3 µA in sleep
Topology: piconet — one central connects to up to 7 peripherals; also BLE mesh
Protocol stack: GATT profiles; iBeacon; BLE mesh
MicroPython boards: Pico W (CYW43439 BT 5.2); ESP32 (BT 4.2)
Best for: smartphone-to-device apps, wearables, beacons, low-power sensors

zones.label: LoRa (Long Range)
zones.summary: Kilometre-range link at very low data rates
facts: Frequency: 915 MHz (US); 868 MHz (Europe); 433 MHz (Asia)
Typical range: 2–5 km urban; 10–20 km rural line-of-sight
Data speed: 0.3–50 kbps (chirp spread spectrum — lower speed = longer range)
Power use: very low — ~40 mA TX; ~1.4 mA RX; < 1 µA sleep
Topology: star (LoRaWAN via gateway) or point-to-point (raw LoRa)
Protocol stack: LoRaWAN for cloud; raw LoRa for direct P2P links
MicroPython boards: needs a LoRa module — RFM95W, SX1276, or LilyGO T3
Best for: agriculture sensors, weather stations, outdoor/remote IoT, long-distance alarms

zones.label: Zigbee 802.15.4
facts: Frequency: 2.4 GHz (global channels 11–26); 868/915 MHz regional channels
Typical range: 10–100 m per hop; mesh extends range across an entire building
Data speed: 250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4 physical layer)
Power use: low — ~30 mA TX; ~1 mA RX; < 1 µA sleep (routers stay on; end devices sleep)
Topology: mesh — devices relay messages for each other; self-healing network
Protocol stack: Zigbee Pro; Zigbee clusters; also used in Matter (Thread)
MicroPython boards: needs an external module — XBee, CC2530, or Zigbee USB coordinator
Best for: smart-home automation, industrial sensors, mesh networks, Home Assistant

quiz.question: Which wireless technology can reach 10–20 km in rural areas at very low data rates?
quiz.explanation: LoRa (Long Range) uses chirp spread spectrum to trade speed for distance, reaching 10–20 km line-of-sight in rural areas — ideal for remote outdoor sensors.

quiz.question: Which wireless technology is built into the Pico W and best for running a web server?
quiz.explanation: WiFi is built into the Pico W (CYW43439) and uses the TCP/IP stack with HTTP/MQTT, making it the right choice for web servers, REST APIs, and cloud IoT.

quiz.question: Which wireless technology uses the least power and is best for a smartwatch talking to a phone?
quiz.explanation: Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) draws only ~15–20 mA transmitting and ~3 µA asleep, making it perfect for wearables and other battery-powered devices that pair with a phone.

quiz.question: Which wireless technology forms a self-healing mesh where devices relay each other's messages?
quiz.explanation: Zigbee (802.15.4) builds a mesh network: each device relays messages for others, so the network self-heals and extends range across a whole building — the backbone of many smart homes.

quiz.question: Which technology is the only one here that requires an external add-on module (it is not built into the Pico W)?
quiz.explanation: LoRa needs an external radio module like the RFM95W or SX1276. WiFi and BLE are built into the Pico W, and while Zigbee also needs a module, LoRa is the one defined by its kilometre range here.
